title: Food & Drink
subtitle: How to eat well in Jordan — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Arabic Coffee (Qahwa)
items.text: Served in tiny cups without sugar, this cardamom-spiced coffee is the lifeblood of Jordanian hospitality. It's traditionally offered at the start of any visit or meal, and refusing it can be considered rude—at least take a few sips.

items.label: Coffee house etiquette
items.text: Qahwas (traditional coffee houses) are male-dominated social spaces where locals spend hours playing backgammon and smoking shisha. Women visitors are welcome but may feel more comfortable in modern cafés; stick to tourist areas if unsure.

items.label: Rainbow Street, Amman
items.text: This bohemian strip in Jabal Amman has excellent modern cafés serving both Arabic coffee and specialty espresso drinks. It's the go-to spot for tourists wanting to experience café culture without the gender-segregated dynamic of traditional qahwas.

items.label: Tea culture
items.text: Mint tea (shai bi na'ana) is ubiquitous and refreshing, especially after heavy meals. It's often served free with meals, and asking for it shows respect for local customs.

items.label: Mansaf
items.text: Jordan's national dish of lamb cooked in a tangy yogurt sauce (jameed) served over rice and pine nuts. This is celebratory food, traditionally eaten with the right hand, and you'll find the best versions at family-run restaurants rather than tourist spots.

items.label: Falafel & Hummus
items.text: These chickpea-based staples are cheaper and fresher than anywhere else you've eaten them—grab falafel sandwiches from street vendors in downtown Amman for under 1 JD. Hummus bil lahma (topped with spiced meat) is a breakfast favorite.

items.label: Zarb
items.text: Lamb or chicken cooked underground in a covered pit, traditionally prepared in Bedouin camps near Petra and Wadi Rum. It's smoky, tender, and worth seeking out; many tour operators include it in evening experiences.

items.label: Maklouba
items.text: A Palestinian-influenced upside-down rice dish with meat and vegetables that's flipped onto the plate—visually dramatic and delicious. It's less common than mansaf but appears on menus throughout the country.

items.label: Knafeh
items.text: A warm dessert of shredded phyllo, melted cheese, and pistachios soaked in sugar syrup—often served at breakfast or after meals. Nablus-style knafeh from the Palestinian territories is considered the gold standard, and you'll find excellent versions in Amman's downtown.

items.label: Tabbouleh & Mezze Platters
items.text: Mixed herb and bulgur salad alongside roasted vegetables, feta, olives, and fresh bread define Levantine dining. Order mezze to share—it's cost-effective and gives you variety without committing to a single main course.

items.label: Mezze comes first
items.text: Traditional meals start with multiple small plates (mezze)—hummus, baba ganoush, fattoush, stuffed grape leaves—before main courses arrive. This is how locals eat; embrace it rather than ordering entrées alone.

items.label: Menu language barrier
items.text: English menus are standard in Amman and tourist areas, but transliterations vary wildly (is it 'shawirma' or 'shawarma'?). Point at dishes other diners are eating or ask your server for recommendations—they're usually eager to help.

items.label: Ask for the bill explicitly
items.text: Servers won't bring the check until you ask (hosbeh); lingering over coffee is expected. Say 'al-hseb min fadlak' (the bill please) when ready to leave.

items.label: Tourist trap warning
items.text: Restaurants in Petra town and near major monuments often inflate prices 50-100% and serve mediocre food. Eat your main meals in nearby towns (Wadi Musa for Petra, Aqaba for Wadi Rum) or ask your hotel for local recommendations.

items.label: Fresh juice stands
items.text: Look for carts and small shops with whole fruits displayed; fresh-squeezed pomegranate, orange, and carrot juices cost 0.5-1 JD and are leagues better than bottled. These are safe, popular with locals, and ubiquitous in city centers.

items.label: Downtown Amman (Balad)
items.text: Crowded, energetic, and full of hole-in-the-wall shawarma joints, falafel stands, and hummus shops where locals eat. This is where you'll find the cheapest, most authentic meals; expect plastic chairs and zero English menus.

items.label: Shmeisani & Jabal Amman restaurants
items.text: Upmarket neighborhoods with sit-down restaurants offering refined Jordanian and international cuisine. Places like Kan Zaman and Al Reef serve mansaf and zarb in pleasant settings; prices are 3-4x downtown but portions are generous.

items.label: Aqaba's waterfront
items.text: Seafood restaurants line the Red Sea coast with fresh fish, prawns, and crab. Al Reef and Berenice are reliable mid-range options; avoid places that grab you aggressively from the street.

items.label: Bedouin camps in Wadi Rum
items.text: Many camps serve traditional zarb cooked in underground pits and Bedouin tea at night—an experience as much as a meal. Book through your tour operator; budget meals are included but quality varies wildly between camps.

items.label: Street food & bakeries
items.text: The best value and often the best flavor: za'atar-topped flatbreads (man'oushe) from early morning bakeries, chicken shawarma wraps from lunch carts, and fresh-baked khubz (pita). These meals cost under 2 JD and fuel most locals' day.

items.label: Alcohol availability
items.text: Jordan is Muslim-majority and conservative; alcohol is available mainly in tourist areas, upscale restaurants, and certain hotels. It's legal but not culturally prominent—you won't find bars on every corner like in Western cities.

items.label: Local beer (Amstel)
items.text: Amstel beer brewed locally is the standard beer in Jordan; it's cheaper than imported brands but widely available in restaurants and shops. A large bottle costs 1.5-3 JD depending on where you buy it.

items.label: Palestinian & Israeli wines
items.text: Several restaurants in Amman stock Palestinian wines (like Cremisan) and Israeli wines, which reflect the region's wine-making tradition. Quality is good, though prices are high by local standards (8-15 JD per glass).

items.label: Soft drinks & fresh juices
items.text: Locally made sodas and fresh juices dominate; Pepsi and Coca-Cola are ubiquitous, but fresh-squeezed fruit juices are cheaper and infinitely better. Jallab (sweet pomegranate and grape molasses drink) is a traditional favorite.

items.label: Respect during Ramadan
items.text: During the Islamic holy month, eating and drinking in public during daylight hours is considered disrespectful. Stick to restaurants and hotels where it's permitted, and avoid eating in front of locals fasting.

items.label: Arabic tea & herbal infusions
items.text: Beyond mint tea, try sage (marwa), chamomile (babonaj), and mixed herb teas—often served free with meals. These are caffeine-free, refreshing, and deeply embedded in daily life.

items.label: Right hand eating
items.text: Always eat with your right hand—using the left is considered offensive and unclean in Islamic tradition. If you're left-handed, do your best; locals will be forgiving of obvious struggle.

items.label: Refusing food politely
items.text: It's impolite to refuse food outright; take a small portion to show respect, then explain dietary restrictions if necessary. Saying 'mashkoor' (thank you) goes a long way in these situations.

items.label: Tipping expectations
items.text: 10% tip is standard at sit-down restaurants; 5-10% for casual spots. Rounding up or leaving small change is acceptable at street vendors. Check if service is already included before tipping.

items.label: Shoes off in homes
items.text: If invited to a Jordanian home for a meal, remove your shoes at the entrance. Compliment the hostess's cooking and take seconds if offered—refusing is insulting.

items.label: Ramadan adjustments
items.text: If dining during Ramadan, be especially respectful of those fasting. Many restaurants shorten hours and reopen at sunset (iftar); evening meals become major social events with special dishes and extended family gatherings.

items.label: Social eating pace
items.text: Meals are social occasions meant to linger over—rushing through is considered rude. Expect to spend 1-2 hours at a sit-down restaurant, especially if invited by locals.
